text: Transrectal natural orifice specimen extraction, a safe and feasible technique in patients undergoing subtotal colectomy. Traditional laparoscopic subtotal colectomy requires a mini-laparotomy or larger than usual ileostomy incision for specimen extraction, which contributes to increased post-operative pain and long-term risk of hernia formation. Transrectal specimen extraction has the potential to reduce this morbidity and improve surgical outcomes. We have performed TR nose in several patients during the last year. This video aims to demonstrate the six steps for TR nose as developed in our institution.

The surgeon completes laparoscopic subtotal colonic mobilization, followed by proximal and distal transection of the specimen. A rectal washout is performed by inserting tubing into the apex of the rectal stump, ensuring thorough cleansing to minimize pelvic cavity contamination. The rectal stump is then opened.

The surgeon inserts a nylon tape via the perineum into the pelvis, followed by a protective plastic sheath, creating a smooth surface for specimen extraction through the rectum. The specimen is inserted into the rectal stump and extracted using a combination of pushing from above and pulling from below. An incision is made in the partially extracted specimen near the anal canal, allowing the insertion of a flatus tube to deflate the specimen for complete extraction.
The surgeon cross-staples the rectal stump and performs an ileostomy, completing the final steps of the procedure.

The surgeon encounters difficulty extracting the bulky transverse colon due to its size. Multiple incisions are made into the colon, followed by sequential decompressions using a flatus tube. The bulky omentum is identified as an obstacle and is carefully dissected off the specimen, allowing the extraction process to continue.
The surgeon addresses gaseous cecal distention, facilitating the successful completion of specimen extraction through the transrectal approach.
